title: Divine Revelation
html:
Big Question

How do we know what is true about God?

There is a scene on the arid slopes of Mount Horeb, in the heart of the Sinai desert, that gives us the key to everything that follows. A man named Moses, a shepherd of sheep, has been fleeing his past for decades. He was a prince in Egypt, but his anger led him to kill an Egyptian, and now he lives in exile, tending someone else's flocks in the land of Midian.

One ordinary day — one of those days when you don't expect anything to change, when everything seems routine — Moses sees something extraordinary: a bush that burns without being consumed. It is not a forest fire. It is a flame that does not destroy what it touches, that does not go out, that burns from within without ever ending. Something that does not fit the laws of nature.

Moses, a hardened shepherd, could have kept walking. Instead, he stops. Scripture says: «I must turn aside and look at this great sight, and see why the bush is not burned up» (Ex 3:3). That curiosity changes everything.

God sees him approaching and calls to him from the bush: «Moses, Moses!» And he answers: «Here I am.» Then God tells him: «Come no closer! Remove the sandals from your feet, for the place on which you are standing is holy ground» (Ex 3:5). And he reveals himself: «I am the God of your father, the God of Abraham, the God of Isaac, and the God of Jacob.»

Moses hides his face, for he is afraid to look at God.

This story is the paradigm of revelation. Notice the dynamic: God takes the initiative — Moses was not looking for God, he was shepherding sheep. God manifests himself in a disconcerting way — a bush that burns without being consumed, something that doesn't fit our categories. God calls Moses by name — not «you» or «mortal,» but «Moses, Moses.» And finally, God entrusts him with a mission — to free his people from Egypt.

This is how God's revelation unfolds throughout history. God is not a puzzle we solve with our intelligence. He is a person who seeks us, calls us by our name, and entrusts us with a mission.

Activity

Can you imagine seeing a bush that burns without being consumed? Moses could have kept walking, but he stopped to look. Sometimes God speaks to us in small things that seem strange or wonderful, and we walk right past them. This week, pay attention: is there something that seems special or catches your attention like the burning bush? It could be a person, a landscape, a song… Stop and look at it. Maybe God is speaking to you.

Draw the burning bush and around it write three things that amaze you about creation.

1. God Reveals Himself

The word «revelation» comes from the Latin revelare, meaning literally «to remove the veil.» Something that was covered is uncovered. But in Christian language, this word has a very precise meaning: it is not that man, through his effort, manages to decipher a mystery. It is that God takes the initiative and makes himself known.

The Catechism of the Catholic Church states it clearly: «God, infinitely perfect and blessed in himself, in a plan of sheer goodness freely created man to make him share in his own blessed life. For this reason, at every time and in every place, God draws close to man. He calls man to seek him, to know him, to love him with all his strength» (CCC 1, which opens the entire Catechism). Revelation is the means by which God accomplishes this plan.

God comes to meet us

The first thing to understand is that revelation is not something man earns or deserves. It is a gift. As the Catechism says: «God, who created man out of love, also calls him to friendship out of love» (CCC 28, summarized in the opening paragraphs). And later, in the chapter on Revelation: «God, who 'dwells in unapproachable light' (1 Tim 6:16), wills to communicate his own divine life to the men he freely created» (CCC 51).

This is the starting point of everything. Revelation is not secret information that God gives us so we can pass a test. It is an invitation to communion. God does not merely reveal things about himself; he reveals himself.

The Catechism highlights three characteristics of this revelation:

  1. It is gradual. God does not reveal everything at once, but adapts to humanity's pace, like a father teaching his child step by step. «God chose to reveal himself and to make known the mystery of his will... over the course of time» (CCC 50).
  2. It adapts to human capacity. God reveals himself «accommodating his words to the nature of human beings» (CCC 50). He does not speak in an incomprehensible heavenly language, but in human words and gestures.
  3. It reveals a plan. Revelation is not a collection of unrelated facts. It has a single purpose: «to make us sharers in the divine nature» (CCC 51, citing 2 Pet 1:4).

Context

Perhaps you have heard the phrase «God reveals himself» and it sounds like overly technical religious language. Think about this: if God exists, and if he is infinite, then he is beyond our natural reach. We cannot climb to heaven to know him like climbing a mountain. If he did not show himself, we would never know him. Every religion, at its core, faces this problem: how to reach a God who infinitely surpasses us?

Christianity's answer is radical: God is not a ceiling we reach, but a Father who comes down the stairs. Revelation is that: God coming to meet us. Not because we deserve it, but because he loves us.

2. The Stages of Revelation

God did not reveal himself all at once. The Catechism describes a process that spans the entire history of salvation, from the origins to its fullness in Christ (CCC 54-64). These are stages of divine pedagogy.

a) From the beginning: Adam, Noah, the patriarchs

«In the beginning, God made himself known to our first parents» (CCC 54). Revelation begins with creation itself: the heavens, the earth, man and woman — everything speaks of God. But humanity, through sin, breaks this communion. God, however, does not abandon man. He promises a redeemer (Gen 3:15) and establishes a covenant with Noah, a new beginning after the flood (CCC 56).

The great figure of this first stage is Abraham. God calls him to leave his land, his kindred, his father's house (Gen 12:1). Abraham does not know where he is going, but he trusts. In him, God begins to form a people: «I will make of you a great nation» (Gen 12:2). Abraham's faith is the model of all faith: responding to God's call without seeing the final destination.

b) The Covenant at Sinai

Revelation takes a huge leap when God liberates Israel from Egypt and makes a covenant with them at Mount Sinai (CCC 62). Here, through Moses, God gives the Law not as a set of arbitrary rules, but as a way of life, an expression of his love and his will. The Law orders the life of the people and prepares them for something greater.

The Sinai covenant is a marriage between God and Israel. Like a husband who commits to his wife, God binds himself to his people indissolubly. And though the people are unfaithful, God remains faithful.

c) The prophets: preparation for the Messiah

«God raised up prophets to prepare his people for salvation» (cf. CCC 64). The prophets are not fortune-tellers predicting a distant future. They are men and women called by God to speak in his name. They denounce the people's infidelity, call for conversion, and announce a new and eternal covenant.

Isaiah, Jeremiah, Ezekiel, Hosea — each prophet reveals an aspect of God's heart. Hosea shows him as a betrayed husband who still loves. Jeremiah as a father with deeply moved entrails. Isaiah as the Holy One who calls his people to holiness.

But all point forward. The most important prophecy is not the prediction of events, but the promise that God himself will come to save his people. «The days are surely coming, says the Lord, when I will make a new covenant with the house of Israel and the house of Judah» (Jer 31:31). The people wait. And the waiting is long. But God keeps his promise.

Connection

The history of Israel may seem distant to you. But think about your own life: how have you come to know God? Was it all at once or little by little? For most of us, God reveals himself gradually: first in the family, then in catechesis, then in personal experiences, in difficult moments, in people who speak to us about him. God's pedagogy with Israel is the same one he uses with you: step by step, adapting to your pace, without forcing, but never giving up.

If you are in a moment of spiritual dryness, remember Israel in the desert. They too had moments of doubt, hunger, thirst. But God did not abandon them. He never does.

3. Jesus Christ: The Full and Definitive Revelation

We have seen the preparatory stages. But what happens when the fullness of time arrives? The Catechism answers clearly: «Christ Jesus, the Son of God made man, is the Father's one, perfect, and unsurpassable Word. In him, the Father has said everything; there will be no other word than the one already spoken» (CCC 65).

There is a story that shows this dramatically: the conversion of St. Paul. Saul of Tarsus, a learned Pharisee, a persecutor of Christians, is on his way to Damascus with letters from the high priest to arrest the followers of Jesus. Suddenly, a light from heaven knocks him to the ground. He hears a voice: «Saul, Saul, why do you persecute me?» And he asks: «Who are you, Lord?» The answer changes world history: «I am Jesus, whom you are persecuting» (Acts 9:5).

Paul was not looking for Jesus. He was persecuting his followers. But Christ finds him, knocks him down, calls him, and transforms him. This is the pattern of revelation in its most radical form: God breaks into a person's life in a way that cannot be ignored. And the person is forever changed.

Why is Christ the definitive revelation?

The author of the Letter to the Hebrews summarizes it masterfully: «Long ago God spoke to our ancestors in many and various ways by the prophets, but in these last days he has spoken to us by a Son» (Heb 1:1-2).

Notice the comparison: the prophets spoke in the name of God. The Son is the Word itself of God made flesh. The prophets announced; the Son is the announcement. The prophets pointed toward the future; the Son is the fullness of time.

This radically distinguishes Christianity from other religions. In Islam, Muhammad is a prophet who receives the Qur'an, a sacred book. In Buddhism, the Buddha teaches a path of enlightenment. In Hinduism, there are many revealed scriptures. But only in Christianity is revelation not a book or a set of teachings: it is a Person. Jesus Christ is not someone who «received» revelation; He is revelation.

The Catechism is emphatic: «The Christian economy, therefore, since it is the new and definitive Covenant, will never pass away; and no new public revelation is to be expected before the glorious manifestation of our Lord Jesus Christ» (CCC 66). This closes the door to any idea of a «later revelation» that surpasses or completes Christ. In him, God said everything. There is nothing more to say.

«After speaking through the prophets, God spoke through the Son. But there is nothing more to say, because what he had said piecemeal through the prophets he has said entirely in the Son. He is the whole word.» St. John of the Cross, Ascent of Mount Carmel II, 22

4. The Transmission of Revelation

If Christ is the full and definitive revelation, how does it reach us, twenty centuries later? The Catechism explains that revelation is transmitted through three channels that are intimately united and cannot be separated (cf. CCC 74-100).

Apostolic Tradition

The apostles received revelation directly from Christ and transmitted it in two ways: orally (preaching, teaching, liturgy) and in writing (the books of the New Testament). This living transmission, which continues in the Church under the assistance of the Holy Spirit, is called Tradition. «Apostolic Tradition is handed on in the Church so that it is not lost in the course of centuries» (CCC 77).

Tradition is not a collection of legends or pious customs. It is the living transmission of the Gospel, which the Church preserves, celebrates, and proclaims in every generation.

Sacred Scripture

The Bible is not just any book. It is the Word of God written under the inspiration of the Holy Spirit. «The divinely revealed realities, which are contained and presented in Sacred Scripture, have been written down under the inspiration of the Holy Spirit» (CCC 105).

This means that God is the principal author of Scripture, but he used human authors with their own styles and characteristics. The Bible is, therefore, truly the word of God and truly the word of men —a wonderful combination of the divine and the human, like the Incarnation itself.

The Magisterium of the Church

Who has the authority to interpret the Word of God authentically? The Catechism answers: «The task of giving an authentic interpretation of the Word of God, whether in its written form or in the form of Tradition, has been entrusted to the living Magisterium of the Church alone» (CCC 85).

The Magisterium is not above the Word of God, but serves it. It does not invent new doctrines; it faithfully guards, interprets, and transmits what was received. The three —Tradition, Scripture, and Magisterium— are so united that none can subsist without the others (CCC 95).

«Sacred Tradition and Sacred Scripture make up a single sacred deposit of the Word of God, entrusted to the Church.» (CCC 97, citing Dei Verbum 10)

Going Deeper

The relationship between Scripture and Tradition was one of the central themes of the Second Vatican Council, treated in the dogmatic constitution Dei Verbum. The Council affirmed that both flow from the same divine source and tend toward the same end (Dei Verbum 9). This overcame a long controversy: the Protestant Reformation held to sola Scriptura (Scripture alone), while the Council of Trent had affirmed that revelation is transmitted through both Scripture and Tradition. Vatican II deepened this teaching by showing their organic unity.

Recommended reading: Dei Verbum (Dogmatic Constitution on Divine Revelation, 1965), especially chapters I and II. Also YouCat 6-12, which offers an accessible version of the same content.

Biblical Connection

Moses was keeping the flock of his father-in-law Jethro, the priest of Midian; he led his flock beyond the wilderness, and came to Horeb, the mountain of God. There the angel of the Lord appeared to him in a flame of fire out of a bush; he looked, and the bush was blazing, yet it was not consumed. Then Moses said, «I must turn aside and look at this great sight, and see why the bush is not burned up.» When the Lord saw that he had turned aside to see, God called to him out of the bush, «Moses, Moses!» And he said, «Here I am.» Then he said, «Come no closer! Remove the sandals from your feet, for the place on which you are standing is holy ground.» He said further, «I am the God of your father, the God of Abraham, the God of Isaac, and the God of Jacob.» And Moses hid his face, for he was afraid to look at God.

— Exodus 3:1-6

This passage is one of the densest in all of Scripture. Every detail is charged with meaning. The bush that burns without being consumed is an image of God himself: he is the fire that purifies but does not destroy, that illuminates but does not consume. The command to remove the sandals indicates that we are standing on holy ground: the encounter with God demands reverence. And God's self-presentation as the God of the patriarchs links this new revelation with previous history —God is not a stranger, he is the same one who called Abraham.

When God says «I AM WHO I AM» (Ex 3:14), he reveals his name, which is a mystery of presence and faithfulness. It is not a name that defines God in human terms, but affirms his absolute existence and sovereign freedom. It is, at the same time, a promise: «I will be with you» (Ex 3:12).

For Reflection

1. Have you ever had an experience where you felt God was seeking you, even though you were not looking for him?

Perhaps it was through a person, a reading, a difficult situation, or an unexpected joy. Revelation does not always come with fire from heaven.

2. What does it tell you that God did not reveal himself all at once, but gradually?

What does it suggest about his patience, his respect for our freedom, and his way of educating us?

3. How does understanding that Christ is the definitive revelation of God change the way you see Jesus?

He is not just a wise teacher or an important prophet. He is the very Word of God made flesh.

4. Do you read the Bible as the Word of God or as an ancient book like any other?

If the Church teaches that it is inspired by the Holy Spirit, what change of attitude should that bring to your reading of it?

Key Takeaway

God is not a puzzle we solve, but a person who speaks to us. Revelation is not secret information, but an invitation to friendship. And Jesus Christ is the definitive word: in him, God has said everything.

Cultural Resources

Iconography The Burning Bush in Byzantine iconography: one of the most beautiful icons of the Eastern tradition depicts the Mother of God as the «Burning Bush.» The icon shows Mary with the child Jesus at the center, surrounded by flames that do not consume her. It is a profound theological image: just as the bush burned without being consumed, Mary contains God without being destroyed.

Painting The Conversion of St. Paul by Caravaggio (1601, Cerasi Chapel, Santa Maria del Popolo, Rome). Caravaggio captures the exact moment Saul falls from the horse, blinded by the light of Christ. The painting is revolutionary for its time: no angels, no visible heavenly glory —just a man thrown to the ground, arms outstretched, the light splitting him in two. It is the image of revelation as an irruption that disarms and transforms.

Art Moses before the Burning Bush by Domenico Fetti (c. 1617, Kunsthistorisches Museum, Vienna). Shows Moses removing his sandals, the flock behind him, the bush in flames. It captures the mix of awe and reverence in the encounter.

Music St. Francis of Assisi's Canticle of the Creatures is a praise of God revealed in creation. The liturgy of Lauds sings it as an acknowledgement that all creation speaks of God. Also Rossini's Moses and the film score from The Ten Commandments.

Literature St. John of the Cross, Ascent of Mount Carmel, Book II, chapter 22: a classic text on Christ as definitive revelation. Also C.S. Lewis, Mere Christianity, where he argues that Christ cannot be merely a moral teacher —he is either a lunatic, a liar, or Lord.

YouCat Questions 5-12 of the YouCat (Youth Catechism): «Why does God reveal himself to man?», «What does Tradition mean?», «What does it mean that the Bible is inspired?» Available at youcat.org.

Catechesis Real + True from OSV News has episodes on revelation and Scripture. Ascension Presents with Fr. Mike Schmitz: «Why the Bible Matters.» In Spanish, Opus Dei offers the series «Temas de fe cristiana» with a theme on Revelation.
